fuzzy_prd: We need a key-derivation toolkit that covers the full chain of operations a self-custody wallet must perform at account setup and recovery time. Starting from raw random bytes, the library should encode them into a human-memorizable word phrase (using a fixed 2048-word English dictionary with a checksum appended before encoding); then it should be able to validate that a phrase a user typed conforms to the allowed word counts and uses only recognized words; then it should stretch a valid phrase into a long binary seed using a standard key-stretching function with a fixed salt string and a fixed iteration count; and finally it should walk a standard hierarchical path through a deterministic key tree derived from that seed to produce both a payment address (in the standard checksum-encoded address format) and the corresponding compressed public key, as well as serialized extended keys for watch-only wallet export. All outputs are stable strings — no randomness after the initial entropy input — so two correct implementations of the same standard must produce identical outputs. Error cases (empty entropy, wrong word count, unrecognized word, non-numeric path component) must come back as predictable machine-readable tags rather than raw exceptions. The derivation path format should accept an optional leading prefix, treat an empty or root-only path as the master key, and support hardened child indices marked with the standard apostrophe notation, consistent with how the extended-key export is handled elsewhere in the wallet module.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: fun entropyToMnemonic(entropy: ByteArray): List<String>; fun validateMnemonic(words: List<String>): ValidationResult; fun mnemonicToSeed(words: List<String>): ByteArray; fun deriveAddress(seed: ByteArray, chain: Chain, index: Int): DerivedAddress { address: String, publicKey: String }; fun deriveExtendedKey(seed: ByteArray, path: String): ExtendedKey { xprv: String, xpub: String }

trigger_keywords: how many iterations for seed derivation
what salt is used in PBKDF2
PBKDF2 parameters for mnemonic to seed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: PBKDF2 uses HMAC-SHA512 as the PRF, exactly 2048 iterations, the space-joined mnemonic phrase (UTF-8) as the password, and the constant ASCII string 'mnemonic' (no spaces, no extra characters) as the salt. The output is 64 bytes (512 bits), rendered as a 128-character lowercase hex string prefixed with 'seed='.

trigger_keywords: which word counts are valid for mnemonic
what are the allowed mnemonic lengths
valid number of words in BIP39 phrase
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Valid word counts are exactly: 12, 15, 18, 21, or 24. Any other count (e.g., 11, 13) emits 'error=invalid_mnemonic_count' followed by 'word_count=<n>'. A count-valid but dictionary-invalid phrase emits 'error=invalid_mnemonic_key' followed by 'word=<first_invalid_word>'.

trigger_keywords: what derivation path is used for addresses
BIP44 path for receive and change
how is chain name mapped to path number
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Address derivation follows BIP44 path m/44'/0'/0'/chain/index where the apostrophe marks hardened derivation. 'receive' maps to chain=0 (external chain), 'change' maps to chain=1 (internal chain). Output is two lines: 'address=<Base58Check P2PKH>' then 'public_key=<66-char lowercase hex of 33-byte compressed key>'.

trigger_keywords: how is entropy length related to word count
how many bits of entropy for 12 words
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: 128 bits of entropy (16 bytes) produces 12 words. The checksum is the leading (entropy_bits / 32) bits of SHA-256 of the raw entropy bytes. Combined bits are split into 11-bit groups, each indexing the 2048-word dictionary. Empty entropy emits 'error=empty_entropy'.

trigger_keywords: what is an invalid path component
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: If any path component (after stripping a trailing apostrophe) is not a valid non-negative integer, derivation emits 'error=invalid_path' followed by 'path=<original-path-string>' (the exact input string verbatim). Empty path, 'm', or '/' is valid and refers to the master key itself.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: consistent with how the extended-key export is handled elsewhere in the wallet module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The deriveExtendedKey function uses the same BIP32 master key construction from seed, then walks the path component by component. The output xprv and xpub strings are BIP32 base58check-serialized extended keys, starting with 'xprv' and 'xpub' respectively.
Base58Check P2PKH (Pay-to-Public-Key-Hash) Bitcoin address format, with version byte 0x00. For example '188TR7fL2MpqoAMez2VgLgsjZoRcttZvAb'.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please refer to the project PRD and the golden implementation under horizontalsystems__unstoppable-wallet-android/golden/ for the authoritative specification.

feature1_entropy_to_mnemonic.json.description: Convert a hexadecimal entropy value into a BIP39-style mnemonic word sentence.

cases.expected_output: jealous digital west actor thunder matter marble marine olympic range dust banner

feature2_validate_mnemonic.json.description: Validate a candidate mnemonic word list.

feature3_mnemonic_to_seed.json.description: Derive the 64-byte binary seed from a mnemonic using PBKDF2-HMAC-SHA512.

feature4_address_derivation.json.description: Derive a payment address and compressed public key at BIP44 path.

feature5_extended_key.json.description: Derive serialized extended keys at a textual derivation path.
